title: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality
display_title: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality
wikitext:
[]: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Wed Jun 26, 2024 6:44 am I know death is a touchy subject for some, so feel free to just ignore this if that's the case for you. Personally, though, I'm really intrigued by it. I think it's because it's the one thing science will never figure out. I don't know. This specific idea of ​​a Black Void after death came to mind this weekend and I did some research on it. I didn't delve too deeply into it, but I did find a few people talking about it and their near-death experiences. Most people described it as a dark “place” with nothing inside but one’s consciousness. There was one guy in particular who described his experience as “a sea of ​​nothingness.” He said it wasn't really water (wasn't wet) but it felt like it. That really stuck with me for some reason. In almost every experience I read, people said they had no bodies (emphasizing the lack of hands as they looked for it), even this guy. Still, he was feeling like he was in some kind of sea. Most of these reports were made by ordinary people, so they did their best to describe their situation as best they could. However, I later found a much more detailed description of someone who was able to really put into words much of what he experienced. He said that after he died and went to this “place” all of his memories appeared in his mind at once and he had to let go of each one of them to become one with nothingness. In the end, he said he didn't know who he was before or even that he was once human. He just knew he had been something that existed. Of course, I'm not saying that life after death exists or doesn't exist. That's up to each person, I'm not going to throw my hat in that ring. Personally, I think these experiences are the result of our conscious minds facing the unconscious (death) and dealing with, dissolving. Maybe that's why some people like it and others don't. People with a stronger sense of “I” (ego) would not want to let go of themselves to become nothing. Who knows? What catches my attention is that the whole thing is very similar to Human Instrumentality if you think about it. This idea of ​​letting go of yourself to become one with nothingness. I always remember that scene in the original ending where everyone explains to Shinji how he needs limitations to be himself. You need to be different from others to know where you end and others begin. They also give this really cool example of how we need the ground, otherwise we would just float around. And this relates to the way I think about Instrumentality. Not only is it a perfect world where anything is possible, but it's all fake. It is also a place where the “I” does not exist, a place where you “die” (for lack of a better term) and become one with everything and therefore with nothingness. A place without boundaries is a place without anything concrete. It isn't a place as much as it is a state of mind, similar to how I see the Black Void. Another thing is that in these reports people always talk about this subtle feeling of “choice” they have between life and death in this state of mind. Almost as if you could feel it without being told it. Again, this reminds me of Instrumentality. I mean, the emotional climax of the series and the movie is Shinji coming to his senses and making that choice. So needless to say, that's just very important to the whole story. I know there's more to this that I haven't cover. As far as I know, this all relates to Buddhism in some way, but I admit I don't know much about it. Still, I find this all very amusing, at least.

[]: Re: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality             Quote Post by asakuraikun » Mon Jul 15, 2024 10:05 pm What makes me a bit skeptical about these near-death experiences is that these people were likely not really dead. Maybe their vitals were off (heartbeat, pupils not reacting to light), but maybe some process in their brains was still on, and they were saved before all ceased indeed. Maybe I'm saying some non-sense and people who knows better about medicine can correct me somewhat. But it still interesting indeed, because I've also seen people state very similar stuff a few times. I have the impression that a person personal religious/spiritual beliefs impact a lot how the person reports the experience. I'm not saying they are lying, but simply that their perception is influenced by it. I'm not a religious person myself (although I was raised a Catholic) but I image these final moments more or less like reported in your text. A sea of nothingness, where the only thing you have access are your most remarkable memories and where there's no other sensations, be it the pain who lead you to death or sound. Maybe this happens in just a few seconds in real world, but seems to take much longer there. I also have the impression that it fades slowly, to the point you can't realize where it ends exactly. Who knows right...

[]: Re: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Wed Jul 24, 2024 10:43 am I actually agree with you. I'm not saying that they're lying, but their experiences could have been their brains preparing themselves for death. I just find particularly interesting how these scenarios deal with nothingness (or the lack of it, in some cases). And that reminds me of how Evangelion handled it. I guess nothingness is a thing. Otherwise we wouldn't have a word for it, we wouldn't refer to it. But this is a paradox. How can the lack of anything be something that exists? And how can our limited human brains interpret it? It's weird to think about.

[]: Re: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality             Quote Post by Axx°N N. » Fri Jul 26, 2024 8:10 pm asakuraikun wrote: What makes me a bit skeptical about these near-death experiences is that these people were likely not really dead.

[]: Re: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality             Quote Post by ChaddyManPrime » Mon Aug 05, 2024 1:05 am It's the ego death, returning to the One/Absolute/Father/Truth/Nous. The existence of non existence, and vice versa. It can be dark and soundless, or bright and loud, sometimes overlapping. I'm pretty positive we go on after this, for how long, or who is right about where we go, I do not know.

[]: Re: Black Void After Death and Human Instrumentality             Quote Post by BernardoCairo » Fri Aug 16, 2024 7:41 pm I know that sounds weird to say, but I think you two have a point and are right. I think each person goes through someting different when dying, even if that something is "nothing" at all.

This specific idea of ​​a Black Void after death came to mind this weekend and I did so
